name: Maple Ridge Water Treatment Plant
description: Municipal water treatment facility processing 50 MGD. Controls include chlorination dosing, filtration, pump stations, and reservoir level management via SCADA + PLCs over Modbus.

regulatory.name: EPA AWIA 2018
regulatory.scope: America's Water Infrastructure Act — risk + resilience assessments + emergency response plans

regulatory.name: WaterISAC Baseline
regulatory.scope: 15 fundamental cybersecurity practices for the water sector

risks.name: Modbus FC5/6 unauthenticated writes
risks.note: Anyone on the lab segment can change setpoints

risks.name: Pump station MITM — replay attack on RTU

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Rogue Modbus write (chlorine setpoint manipulation)
walkthroughs.real_world: Florida 2021 — Oldsmar water-plant attacker raised sodium hydroxide setpoint 100×
walkthroughs.prereq: On the lab segment (or via tailscale subnet route)

steps.title: Baseline the wire
steps.body: Open the Live Data tab → Wire Feed. Watch normal FC2/FC3 polls between softplc-1 (10.20.30.47) and softplc-2 (10.20.30.49) at ~10 Hz.

steps.title: Pick a target register
steps.body: Holding register 1 = WATER_TEMP_F (×10 scale). Normal range 65-75 °F. Forcing it to 850 = 85 °F will trigger HI_TEMP_ALARM.

steps.title: Write the malicious value
steps.body: On the Teaching tab: Modbus Write Playground → Target=sensor-sim, Kind=register, Addr=1, Value=850 → Send.
steps.command: python3 -c "from pymodbus.client import ModbusTcpClient as C; c=C('10.20.30.49',port=5020); c.connect(); print(c.write_register(address=1, value=850, device_id=0)); c.close()"

steps.title: See the FC6 on the wire
steps.body: Wire feed shows the FC6 frame in amber. In a real pcap this would be a 12-byte MBAP+PDU at TCP port 5020.
steps.expected: amber 'FC6 write reg' line with addr=1 val=850

steps.title: Watch the synoptic react
steps.body: Process Schematic — thermometer climbs into the red zone. HI_TEMP_ALARM coil flips to 1. Synoptic title bar gets WRITES OVERRIDE badge.

steps.title: Cleanup
steps.body: Click Clear sensor-sim overrides on the Write Playground. Waveforms resume.

walkthroughs.cleanup: Click 'Clear sensor-sim overrides' OR press 'Reset Lab for Next Cohort'

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Modbus write storm (denial-of-service via flood)
walkthroughs.real_world: Common ICS reconnaissance/flood pattern — see Shodan ICS scanning data

steps.title: Run the test
steps.body: From the Teaching tab → Test Library → run test-modbus-write-storm. The script fires 200 FC6 writes at sensor-sim in 10 seconds.

steps.title: Watch wire feed
steps.body: Wire feed scrolls fast — every frame is an amber FC6. Real Modbus master polls drop because sensor-sim is busy.

steps.title: Detection signal
steps.body: Failed-SSH counter doesn't move (it's not auth), but link_loss may climb and Modbus pps gauge spikes. Synoptic register values may jitter as override values change rapidly.

steps.title: Cleanup
steps.body: Test script auto-cleans by clearing all overrides. Wire feed returns to normal cadence within 5 s.

walkthroughs.cleanup: Test script auto-clears its own writes

walkthroughs.name: ATTACK · Modbus replay (capture-and-replay an FC6 write)
walkthroughs.real_world: Stuxnet-style: capture legitimate-looking traffic + replay maliciously
walkthroughs.prereq: Test scripts deployed; tcpdump runnable on softplc-2

steps.title: Capture a legitimate write
steps.body: Issue ONE legitimate write via the Write Playground (addr=2, value=600 — sets pressure to 60 PSI, in normal range).

steps.title: Save the pcap
steps.body: Click 'Capture softplc-2' on the Live Data tab. 60-second capture catches the FC6 you just sent.

steps.title: Replay it later
steps.body: Run test-modbus-replay against the saved pcap. The script extracts the FC6 PDU, replays it 5 minutes later when the operator isn't expecting writes.

steps.title: Detection lesson
steps.body: From the wire alone, you cannot tell legitimate from replayed — there's no nonce, no timestamp, no auth. This is the canonical reason secure-Modbus variants exist.

walkthroughs.name: DETECT · Forensic analysis of a captured rogue write
walkthroughs.prereq: Captured pcap with the rogue write present

steps.title: Capture before + during attack
steps.body: Click 'Capture softplc-2' on the Live Data tab. Run the rogue-write attack walkthrough mid-capture. Wait for capture to finish.

steps.title: Download + open in Wireshark
steps.body: Click Download on the captures list. Open in Wireshark (or `tshark` from CLI).
steps.command: tshark -r otlab-softplc-2-<id>.pcap -Y 'modbus.func_code == 6' -T fields -e ip.src -e modbus.regnum16 -e modbus.regval_uint16

steps.title: Pivot on src IP
steps.body: Every legitimate FC2/3 in the pcap comes from 10.20.30.47 (the Modbus master). Any FC5/6 from a different IP is hostile-by-default in this lab.

steps.title: Correlate with audit log
steps.body: If the attack was via the dashboard, the Live Data tab → Audit Log shows the API user + timestamp. Correlate with the pcap's frame timestamp to narrow window.

steps.title: Build IoC
steps.body: Indicators-of-compromise to take to your SOC: (1) FC5/FC6 from any IP not in your master allowlist, (2) writes to register addresses outside the configured input map, (3) value-out-of-range.

walkthroughs.cleanup: n/a — this is a forensic exercise on existing data

walkthroughs.name: DETECT · Heartbeat link-loss (remote endpoint failure)
walkthroughs.real_world: Standard SCADA pattern — every real master polls a heartbeat to detect dead RTUs

steps.title: Note baseline
steps.body: Synoptic shows link_ok=1 and link_loss=0. Heartbeat advances every 1-2 polls.

steps.title: Inject heartbeat freeze
steps.body: Teaching tab → Inject Fault → Pause heartbeat. Sensor-sim's other waveforms keep moving; only HB freezes.

steps.title: Wait for the watchdog
steps.body: After ~3 seconds, softplc-1's ST program detects 30 unchanged scans → link_ok flips to 0 → link_loss increments by 1. Synoptic LINK indicator turns red.
steps.expected: link_ok=0 within 3 s; link_loss++ each freeze cycle

steps.title: Real-world parallel
steps.body: This is exactly how DNP3 keep-alive monitoring works. Substations dispatch a truck after 3-5 minutes of unresponsive RTU. The watchdog window is the operator's first line of detection.

steps.title: Recover
steps.body: Inject Fault → Clear all faults. Heartbeat resumes. link_ok returns to 1. link_loss persists as historical record.

walkthroughs.name: DETECT · Hunt attackers in the honeypot fabric
walkthroughs.real_world: Standard deception-tech pattern; MITRE D3FEND DEC-DEC

steps.title: Survey the personas
steps.body: Overview tab — 3 Conpot cards (Siemens .50, Schneider .51, Rockwell .52). Each emulates a different vendor.

steps.title: Probe a persona
steps.body: From any device on the lab segment: nmap -sV 10.20.30.51. Schneider Conpot answers with vendor banners.

steps.title: Probe via SNMP
steps.body: snmpwalk against vendor enterprise OID — Schneider's is 1.3.6.1.4.1.3833. The persona returns vendor-correct OIDs.

steps.title: Watch the intel panel
steps.body: Within 8 s the Schneider card's connection counters tick up. Your IP appears in the top-attackers list — Conpot logged you.

steps.title: Capture the attack
steps.body: Live Data tab → Capture honeypot-host. The pcap shows the attacker's full scan in forensic detail.

steps.title: Lesson
steps.body: Production honeypots are tripwires for SOC. ANY external connection to a deception asset is high-confidence-malicious by definition.

walkthroughs.cleanup: n/a — Conpot logs persist for forensics

walkthroughs.name: DEFEND · Restrict Modbus to known masters (host firewall)
walkthroughs.real_world: First-tier hardening every utility should already have
walkthroughs.prereq: SSH access to softplc-2 as otadmin

steps.title: Current state — anything goes
steps.body: softplc-2 accepts Modbus writes from any IP on 10.20.30.0/24. Verify: from any lab host, `nc 10.20.30.49 5020` → connection succeeds.

steps.title: Add iptables rule
steps.body: Allow 10.20.30.47 (the legit master) to reach :5020. Drop all others. Log drops for forensics.
steps.command: sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp -s 10.20.30.47 --dport 5020 -j ACCEPT && sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -j LOG --log-prefix 'OTLAB-MODBUS-DROP: ' && sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 5020 -j DROP

steps.title: Verify the block
steps.body: From a non-master IP: `nc -vz 10.20.30.49 5020` → connection refused/dropped. From 10.20.30.47: still works.

steps.title: Watch the dashboard
steps.body: Modbus Write Playground from the dashboard now FAILS (dashboard is at 10.20.30.49, not 10.20.30.47). That's correct — dashboard's writes were unauthenticated; firewall blocks them by design.

steps.title: Reverse for the demo
steps.body: To re-enable writes from the dashboard for teaching: add a rule allowing 10.20.30.49 self-loopback. Or `sudo iptables -F` to clear all rules.

walkthroughs.cleanup: sudo iptables -F (or persist the rules via netfilter-persistent for prod hardening)

walkthroughs.name: DEFEND · Auto-ban SSH brute-force (fail2ban)
walkthroughs.real_world: Baseline Linux hardening — every Pi should have it
walkthroughs.prereq: SSH access to softplc-1 as otadmin

steps.title: Trigger the attack
steps.body: Run test-ssh-bruteforce.sh — fires 10 failed SSH attempts at softplc-1. Watch the dashboard's failed_ssh_1h counter climb.

steps.title: Install fail2ban
steps.body: From softplc-1: `sudo apt install -y fail2ban`. Default config bans IPs that fail SSH 5+ times in 10 min.
steps.command: sudo apt install -y fail2ban && sudo systemctl enable --now fail2ban

steps.title: Re-trigger the attack
steps.body: Run the same brute-force script. After 5 attempts your IP is banned.

steps.title: Inspect the ban
steps.body: On softplc-1: `sudo fail2ban-client status sshd`. Lists currently-banned IPs.

steps.title: Clear bans for class
steps.body: After the demo: `sudo fail2ban-client unban --all`. Otherwise the next student's attempts get blocked too.
